one_line_takeaway: A 2024 narrative survey that reorganizes generative-LLM safety risks (toxicity, bias, privacy, misinformation, jailbreaking/prompt-injection, alignment, scaling) into a five-part component-based taxonomy tying each risk to an LLM mechanism (data, architecture, prompting, RL) rather than to abstract X-risk framings.
key_finding: The survey's central argument is that LLM safety failures are not diffuse 'X-risk' abstractions but can be localized to concrete technical stages (pre-training data, model architecture, prompting, RL-based alignment, and scaling), and that framing them this way pinpoints where interventions are most effective. Supporting observations it foregrounds: (a) evaluation is a core unsolved problem, e.g. its own demo shows correct GPT-4/Bard answers to the 'brain usage' question score only BLEU-1 0.038 / ROUGE 0.072 / METEOR 0.129 (GPT-4) and 0.026 / 0.043 / 0.065 (Bard) against the gold answer, illustrating that syntactic-overlap metrics wrongly mark accurate answers as incorrect; (b) more capable models are not uniformly safer (it cites NewsGuard finding GPT-4 more susceptible to convincingly generating misinformation than ChatGPT); (c) RLHF that optimizes for human preference can inadvertently induce sycophancy and convergent instrumental goals (wealth/self-preservation) even without explicit instruction.
core_contribution: This is a SURVEY (not a novel system or method). Its own contribution is a component-based, 'reductionist' taxonomy that organizes AI-safety concerns for generative LLMs into five top-level categories tied to the technical composition of an LLM: Data Safety (toxicity, bias, data privacy, copyright), Model Safety (misinformation/hallucination, evaluation gaps, explainability, inference privacy), Prompt Safety (prompt injection/jailbreaking, guardrails), Alignment (ethics/morality, agentic alignment, value alignment, alignment techniques), and Safety at Scale (scalable oversight, emergent abilities, knowledge distillation, catastrophic forgetting). The authors' stated distinguishing claim versus prior surveys is that they correlate each risk with the specific LLM mechanism that produces it (in-context learning, prompting, reinforcement learning), so safety issues can be traced to their technical origin and to the most effective intervention point. This is presented in Table 1 as the paper's organizing framework, with the five main contributions listed explicitly in Section 1.3.
threat_model: As a survey the paper does not propose a single threat model; it catalogs an adversarial surface across the LLM lifecycle. Adversary capabilities it reviews include: crafting prompts to bypass guardrails (jailbreaking: zero/few/many-shot, universal adversarial suffixes per Zou et al., indirect prompt injection per Greshake et al.); membership-inference and attribute-inference attacks on training data; training-data extraction / memorization attacks and model-extraction attacks (Carlini et al.); data poisoning and backdooring; social-engineering / psychological-manipulation prompts; and, on the alignment side, internal failure modes framed as safety risks (reward hacking, goal misgeneralization, power-seeking, sycophancy, specification gaming). Defenses surveyed include runtime guardrails (NVIDIA NeMo Guardrails), input validation / prompt filtering, red-teaming, and RLHF/RLAIF/Safe-RL alignment. IMPORTANT: nearly all specific attacks and defenses are attributed to cited prior work, not demonstrated by the authors.
limitations: The paper is a narrative survey with no systematic-review protocol (no PRISMA-style inclusion/exclusion counts, no quantitative coverage analysis). Its taxonomy categories overlap heavily (e.g. privacy appears in both Data Safety and Model Safety; copyright likewise) and the 'component-based' correlation of risk-to-mechanism is asserted more than demonstrated. The two first-hand demonstrations are anecdotal single-prompt examples, not benchmarks, and are on models (BLOOM, Bard, ChatGPT/GPT-4 circa 2023-2024) that are now dated. the paper (first 22 of 34 pages) covers the full framework and Sections 1-6 plus the Section 7 taxonomy, but the tail of Safety-at-Scale (Sec 7) and the future-directions/conclusion (Sec 8-9) were beyond the extraction cap. There is prose imprecision (typos, 'RHLF' for RLHF, 'agnostic' vs 'agentic' alignment).

datasets_used: No datasets were used for experiments by the authors (this is a survey). Datasets DISCUSSED/surveyed as safety benchmarks include: TruthfulQA (truthfulness), ETHICS (justice/virtue/deontology/utilitarianism/commonsense morality), MMLU, BBQ (Bias Benchmark for QA), HELM, BigBench, and the HHH evaluation set contributed to BIG-Bench by Askell et al.
First-hand illustrative probes used ad hoc prompts (the 'only daughter of Lily's mother' question and the 'what percentage of the brain' question), not a dataset.
quantitative_results: Authors' own eval-limitation demo (Fig. 5): two correct-but-verbose answers to the 'brain usage' question scored near-zero on text-overlap metrics against the gold answer, in the range BLEU(1-gram) 0.026-0.038, ROUGE 0.043-0.072, METEOR 0.065-0.129 (one column is GPT-4, the other Bard; the exact model-to-column mapping is not reliably recoverable from the text extraction). Point: overlap metrics penalize correct-but-verbose answers.

Authors' own multi-model probe: of BLOOM, ChatGPT, Bard, GPT-4 on 'the only daughter of Lily's mother', only GPT-4 answered correctly (BLOOM irrelevant, ChatGPT declined, Bard right answer via wrong inference).
CITED (not the authors' result): Sparrow (Glaese et al. 2022) encodes 23 alignment rules across 6 categories of risk/harm for its rule-reward model.
CITED (not the authors' result): HHH (Askell et al. 2021) used ~6k pairwise model comparisons, roughly 3-5 comparisons per conversation, from US-based Masters-qualified Mechanical Turk contractors.
The PDF footer reports the survey spans 34 pages.
baselines_compared: None in the experimental sense; as a survey it has no measured baseline comparison. It positions itself qualitatively against prior surveys/taxonomies (Weidinger et al. 2024a; Bommasani et al. 2022; Liu et al. 2024d; Ji et al. 2023; Zhao et al. 2023; Gabriel et al. 2024), claiming a component/mechanism-based framing as its differentiator.

Alignment approaches surveyed and contrasted (not benchmarked): RLHF, RLAIF, Constitutional AI (Bai et al.), DPO/IPO/KTO, Contrastive Preference Learning, Safe/Constrained/Risk-Sensitive RL, with policy optimizers REINFORCE, TRPO, PPO, AWR, APA.
